subjectLine: Microglia seed Alzheimer's plaques — not just clear them 🧠
borisTake: Microglia seeding amyloid is a proper paradigm shift — these aren't passive bystanders, they're co-manufacturers of the pathology we've been trying to clear. This reshapes how I think about neuroinflammation protocols and makes senolytic targeting of dysfunctional microglia look a lot more urgent.
leadAnalysis: Researchers at VIB and Uniklinikum Erlangen have demonstrated that microglia don't only fail to clear amyloid-β — they actively remodel soluble Aβ42 into extracellular fibrils with high seeding activity, the same structures found in Alzheimer's patients' brains. The mechanism is perverse: microglia's normal phagocytic machinery, confronted with protein aggregates it cannot degrade, amplifies the problem by converting soluble peptides into highly contagious fibrillar seeds. This has direct implications for the 'activated microglia' phenotype increasingly targeted by senolytics and TREM2 agonists — we may need to intervene before the seeding cascade begins rather than after. The finding also helps explain persistent failures in passive immunotherapy approaches that cleared existing plaques without preventing new seeding.
oneNumber: 30% | Increase in mouse grip strength from a single gut bacterium — Roseburia inulinivorans — oral supplementation

papers.title: Microglia Seed Alzheimer's Plaques — Not Just Clear Them
papers.summary: Cell studies show microglia actively remodel soluble Aβ42 into fibrillar seeds with high seeding activity — the same structures found in Alzheimer's brains. Flips the prevailing model of microglia as passive defenders against plaque buildup.

papers.title: Mouse Whole Brain Vitrified and Rewarmed With Preserved Neuronal Function
papers.summary: Using cryoprotective agents and rapid vitrification, researchers froze mouse brain slices and a complete brain — upon rewarming, much of the neuronal function was preserved. First demonstration of functional recovery after whole-brain cryopreservation.

papers.title: Roseburia inulinivorans Boosts Muscle Strength ~30% in Mice
papers.summary: A single gut bacterial species correlates with muscle strength across grip, leg press, and bench press in humans. Oral supplementation in mice increased forelimb grip strength ~30%, with metabolomic changes pointing to muscle fibre type shifts and purine pathway activation. The population declines with age.

papers.title: Resistance Training Slows Brain Aging in 309-Person RCT
papers.summary: Brain clock models analyzing MRI data from 309 adults (62–70 years) found that both heavy and moderate resistance training over one year measurably slowed brain aging. Effect held across intensities — moderate was comparable to heavy.

papers.title: 7-Ketocholesterol Is More Dangerous Than Previously Appreciated
papers.summary: Matthew O'Connor et al. publish a detailed review of how 7-ketocholesterol (7KC), an oxidized cholesterol, drives multi-tissue dysfunction. Relevant to Cyclarity's cyclodextrin-based clearance approach and the broader atherogenesis/longevity agenda.

papers.title: ATP Deficiency in Synapses Drives Parkinson's-Like Protein Aggregation
papers.summary: ATP deficiency disrupts dopamine processing in synapses, leading to accumulation of the harmful protein species characteristic of Parkinson's disease. ATP supplementation was partially protective, opening a mitochondrial angle on neurodegeneration.

papers.title: PUFA:MUFA Ratio Governs T-Cell Viability and Anti-Tumor Immunity
papers.summary: The ratio between polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fatty acids affects T-cell viability, humoral immunity, and anti-tumor immune responses via ferroptosis pathways. Dietary fat composition turns out to matter substantially for immune longevity.
